Table 2: Accuracy and order stability metrics of LLMs with different training methods. +/− indicates improvement or degradation relative to the SFT baseline. ↑ indicates that higher values of this metric represent better model accuracy or stronger order stability, ↓ indicates that lower values of this metric represent lower model hallucination.
Figure 5: Comparison examples of PAFT and DGAO test results.
Table 8: Hyperparameter α sweeping experiments (using Llama-3.2-3B as the base model).

Table 7: Accuracy and order stability metrics of LLMs with different training methods. +/− indicates improvement or degradation relative to the SFT baseline. ↑ indicates that higher values of this metric represent better model accuracy or stronger order stability, ↓ indicates that lower values of this metric represent lower model hallucination.

Figure 2: DGAO algorithm framework. A batch contains M (e.g., M = 2 in the figure) queries, each query consists of text prompts and Q. Each text prompt is constructed into N order variants, and rewards are obtained through respective inference. Based on the rewards, intra-group relative advantage Aind and inter-group relative advantage Agroup are calculated respectively, and the aggregated final advantage Afinal is obtained to guide parameter updates.
